name: Neutrino mass smallness
summary: Neutrino masses are at most ~0.1 eV (sum cosmological bound; oscillation splittings ~0.05 eV), at least 10^6 below the electron mass and 10^11 below the electroweak scale. With Dirac masses generated by the Higgs, this would require Yukawa couplings y_nu ~ 10^-12 - 10^-13, far smaller than any other SM Yukawa.

characteristic_numbers.note: Even relative to the lightest charged fermion, neutrinos are extremely light.

principles_stressed.note: Tiny Dirac Yukawas are technically natural (small values protected by chiral symmetry) but the gap relative to charged-lepton Yukawas is unexplained. Majorana masses via the Weinberg dimension-5 operator naturally suppress m_nu by v/Lambda; a high Lambda ~ 10^14 GeV gives the right scale, hinting at heavy right-handed neutrinos.

history: Neutrino oscillations established mass differences (Super-Kamiokande 1998, SNO 2001). Type-I seesaw (Minkowski 1977; Yanagida 1979; Gell-Mann-Ramond-Slansky 1979; Mohapatra-Senjanovic 1980) is the textbook explanation. KamLAND-Zen and similar experiments probe the Majorana nature; cosmological bounds tighten the absolute scale.

references.citation: Minkowski, 'mu -> e gamma at a rate of one out of 10^9 muon decays?', Phys. Lett. B 67, 421 (1977)
Yanagida, 'Horizontal symmetry and masses of neutrinos', Conf. Proc. C7902131, 95 (1979)
Gell-Mann, Ramond, Slansky, 'Complex spinors and unified theories' (1979), in Supergravity, ed. P. van Nieuwenhuizen and D. Z. Freedman
